name: Astragalus (Huang Qi)
description: A foundational TCM root herb used to tonify Qi, particularly Spleen and Lung Qi. Traditionally used for fatigue, low immunity, and shortness of breath. Often taken as a tea or in soups.
cautions: Generally well tolerated; avoid during acute infections per TCM theory (may trap pathogens).

name: Ginseng (Ren Shen)
description: A warming root tonic used to strongly replenish Qi and support the body during exhaustion, recovery from illness, or chronic fatigue. Considered one of the most powerful Qi tonics in TCM.
preparation: Decoction or chewed slices, 3-9g per day
cautions: Avoid with heat-pattern presentations (red tongue, irritability) or alongside stimulants.

name: Moringa (Moringa oleifera)
description: Known across the Caribbean as a highly nutritive leaf used to combat fatigue and general weakness, often brewed as a tea or added to soups. Rich in iron and considered supportive for low energy and anemia-like presentations.
preparation: Leaf tea, 1-2 cups per day, or dried leaf powder added to food
cautions: Generally safe; very high doses may have a mild laxative effect.

name: Soursop Leaf (Graviola)
description: Widely used in Caribbean folk medicine as a calming tea, traditionally taken in the evening to ease tension and support sleep. Associated with cooling overly 'heated' or agitated states.
preparation: Leaf tea (a few dried leaves steeped in hot water), taken in the evening
cautions: Traditional use is generally for occasional/short-term use; not typically used long-term in high doses.

name: Fever Grass (Lemongrass)
description: One of the most common Caribbean household remedies, used as a tea for colds, fevers, and digestive discomfort. Traditionally taken hot to induce sweating and 'break' a fever.
preparation: Fresh or dried leaf/stalk tea, taken hot
cautions: Generally very safe and widely used; mild diuretic effect.

name: Ginger (Sheng Jiang / Ginger Root)
description: Used in both TCM and Caribbean traditions as a warming root that supports digestion, dispels 'cold' presentations (cold limbs, poor circulation), and helps move stagnant Qi. Common in teas and cooking across both traditions.
preparation: Fresh root tea (sliced and steeped), or added to food
cautions: Avoid large amounts with heat-pattern presentations (red tongue, night sweats) or active bleeding disorders.

name: Cordyceps
description: A TCM tonic traditionally used to support Kidney Yang and overall vitality, particularly for cold limbs, low stamina, and slow recovery. Often used by those recovering from prolonged illness.
preparation: Added to soups or taken as a powder, 3-9g per day
cautions: Warming in nature; avoid with heat-pattern presentations.

name: Rehmannia (Shu Di Huang)
description: A classic TCM herb for nourishing Yin and Blood, traditionally used for night sweats, dry mouth, and a red tongue with little coating. Often included in formulas for chronic depletion.
preparation: Decoction, typically combined with other herbs in formulas, 9-15g
cautions: Avoid with dampness or digestive weakness (can be heavy/cloying for weak digestion).

name: Cerasee (Bitter Melon Vine)
description: A very bitter Caribbean tea traditionally used as a 'cleansing' tonic, often associated with clearing heat and dampness, supporting digestion, and addressing skin eruptions linked to 'heat' in the blood.
preparation: Vine and leaf tea, steeped and strained due to bitterness, taken in small amounts
cautions: Very bitter; traditionally used in moderation. Caution with hypoglycemia, as it may lower blood sugar.

name: Poria (Fu Ling)
description: A TCM fungus used to drain dampness and support the Spleen, often for presentations of bloating, heaviness, and a swollen or tooth-marked tongue. Mild and commonly combined with other Spleen-supporting herbs.
preparation: Decoction, typically 9-15g, often in formula combinations
cautions: Very gentle; few cautions, though excessive use may be drying over long term.

name: Hibiscus (Sorrel)
description: Known in the Caribbean as 'sorrel,' this tart red flower tea is traditionally associated with cooling the body and supporting circulation. Often consumed seasonally and considered helpful for 'hot' presentations.
preparation: Dried flower tea, steeped, often sweetened lightly
cautions: May have a mild effect on blood pressure; those on blood pressure medication should use cautiously.

name: Salvia / Danshen (Red Sage Root)
description: A TCM root herb traditionally used to invigorate Blood and resolve stasis, associated with a purple tongue or dark spots. Often used to support circulation.
cautions: Avoid combining with blood-thinning medications without professional guidance, as both may affect circulation/clotting.

name: Noni
description: Used in some Caribbean and broader tropical folk traditions as a tonic associated with supporting circulation and general resilience, often taken as a juice or tea despite its strong taste/smell.
preparation: Fruit juice or tea, small daily amounts
cautions: Strong flavor; some traditional use cautions around liver sensitivity with very high, long-term consumption.

name: Schisandra (Wu Wei Zi)
description: A TCM 'five-flavor' berry traditionally used to nourish Yin, calm the mind, and support resilience to stress. Often used for presentations involving fatigue combined with irritability or poor sleep.
preparation: Decoction or small amounts of dried berries, 3-9g
cautions: Avoid with acute heat-pattern presentations or excess stomach acid issues.

name: Cinnamon Bark (Rou Gui / Caribbean Cinnamon Tea)
description: Used in TCM to warm and tonify Yang, particularly for cold limbs and low circulation, and similarly used in Caribbean households as a warming tea for colds and poor circulation.
preparation: Small amounts of bark steeped in hot water, or added to other teas
cautions: Warming; avoid with heat-pattern presentations, fevers, or during pregnancy in concentrated amounts.
